summary: An extended digression discusses aggadic maxims: that sinful thoughts are worse than the sin itself (with a mnemonic about meat smell), that fever in winter is worse than in summer, and that the morning star (netz hachama) is stronger than moonlight. Rabbi Abbahu derives from Psalm 22 that Esther is compared to the morning star - the last miracle before dawn. The page then addresses what happened when the tamid was slaughtered before dawn: the ruling and a key principle about day-slaughter requirements.

glossary.en: Sinful thought; the Gemara teaches that contemplating a sin is worse than the sin itself in some respects.
Sunrise; the morning star's light, which the Gemara considers stronger and more dangerous than moonlight.
Morning star; the bright star visible just before dawn, analogized to Esther as the last miracle.

rashiTranslations.en: Sinful thoughts are worse than the sin itself - mnemonic: smell of meat.
Fever in winter is worse than in summer - mnemonic: heating a cold oven.
Rabbi Abbahu: the morning star is stronger than moonlight - from Psalm 22:1.
Esther is compared to the morning star - the last of all miracles.
Why isn't Hanukkah the last miracle? Hanukkah was after Purim.
Purim was not the conclusion of all miracles for all Jews - Hanukkah was the last universal miracle.
Mishna: they slaughtered the tamid before dawn - what is the ruling?
The incident occurred during the rest of the year (not Yom Kippur) - not a Yom Kippur ruling.
Father of R. Avin: a daily offering slaughtered before dawn is invalid.
Winter fever is more intense than summer fever.
Because the body and air are cold; the illness heats it from within.
The sign: a cold oven - needs much wood to heat; winter fever similarly works against cold body.
A cold oven requires much wood - similarly winter fever must work harder to heat a cold body.
The sign: a cold oven.
The illness must work harder against a cold body.
Re-learning something forgotten is harder than learning something new.
One who learned, became distracted, and forgot finds it hard to re-learn.
More so than learning something never studied.
The sign: clay from old clay - mixing mortar from old clay.
Is harder than mixing fresh earth clay.
Because old material is harder to blend.
What is R. Yehuda's reasoning for saying the sun column splits?
This doe - not exactly a doe since a doe has no horns.
Why is Esther compared to a morning doe?
In tractate Megilla (15b) we are told Esther said this psalm.
When she entered the idol chamber the Shechina left her.
She said 'my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'
This fits well according to the view that says - there is a dispute in Megilla.
One view says it [Esther] makes hands impure [as sacred scripture].
And one view says it does not.
He aligns it with R. Binyamin bar Yefet's interpretation.
Who derives 'morning star' as R. Binyamin bar Yefet does.
Why are the prayers of the righteous compared to a doe?
This 'shachar' is interpreted as prayer.
As in 'my spirit within me I seek You at dawn' (Is. 26).
'For the conductor of the morning' - prayer is like a doe.
Each year as its horns grow, they branch further.
When is this? We assumed 'they brought the KG down to the immersion chamber' is the end of the Mishna.
And that on the day they erred and slaughtered the tamid at night.
They brought the KG down to the immersion chamber to slaughter another tamid after immersion.
When did this error occur? If we say on a regular year - not Yom Kippur.
At end of a month when the moon rises just before dawn.
A KG is not needed - other days' service does not require a KG.
Can moonlight appear just before dawn? The premise is questioned.
The conclusion: on Yom Kippur, when the observer said 'Berkai' etc., they took him to the burning place.
And 'they brought the KG down' is a separate statement, about the Yom Kippur procedure.
Teaching the order of Yom Kippur service: when the observer says 'Berkai' they bring the KG down.
For the morning immersion on Yom Kippur.
Not only this sacrifice - not only the slaughter at night was burnt.
But also bird-neckings and flour-pinchings done at night are burnt.
Because they are derived from animal slaughter - bird neckings and flour pinchings.
Take the place of animal slaughter.
They are derived from animal slaughter; what happened happened - they cannot be restored.
The tanna teaches it and answers it from his own reasoning.
He himself teaches it and gives this explanation.
He.

display.whats: Three aggadic maxims about things worse than their counterparts (sinful thought, winter fever, morning star). Rabbi Abbahu: Esther = the morning star, the last great miracle of the Jewish people before the 'dawn' of messianic redemption.
display.hint: The morning star discussion (apropos the dawn theme of the previous page) spirals into a meditation on Esther and the nature of Jewish history's last miracle before full redemption.

learning.summary: Three memorable aggadic maxims: (1) Sinful thoughts are worse than the sin itself, like the smell of meat which drives hunger more than the taste. (2) Fever in winter is more dangerous than in summer, like heating a cold oven. (3) The morning star's light (netz hachama) is more intense than moonlight. From the third maxim, R. Abbahu derives that Psalm 22 - 'For the choirmaster, on the morning star' - was said by Esther about herself: she was the morning star, the last miracle performed for the Jewish people. The Gemara then asks: wasn't Hanukkah later? Answer: Purim was the last miracle for ALL Jews everywhere, while Hanukkah was for those in Eretz Yisrael only.
learning.ahaMoment: Esther as the 'morning star' is not just a poetic comparison - it maps her historically: just as the morning star is the last brilliant light before full dawn, Esther's miracle was the last great redemption before the eventual messianic dawn.
learning.memoryAnchor: Esther = morning star = last darkness before dawn. The pattern: most intense right before the end.
learning.openQuestion: If Purim is the 'last miracle' for all Jews, does the Gemara hold that no miracle will occur for all Jews until the messianic era? Where does the Holocaust or the founding of the State of Israel fit?

argumentFlow.label: Sinful thoughts worse than sin
argumentFlow.text: Sinful thoughts are more harmful than the sin itself - the smell of meat stimulates appetite more than the taste. The mental craving is more destructive than the act.

argumentFlow.label: Winter fever worse than summer fever
argumentFlow.text: A fever in winter is more dangerous than in summer - heating a cold oven takes more fuel and effort, so a winter fever burns hotter inside the body.

argumentFlow.label: Morning star stronger than moonlight
argumentFlow.text: R. Abbahu: the netz hachama (morning star's light) is stronger and more intense than moonlight, as derived from Psalm 22.

argumentFlow.label: Esther = morning star, last miracle
argumentFlow.text: Psalm 22 begins 'For the choirmaster, on the morning star' - Esther recited this Psalm about herself. She was the 'morning star': the last divine miracle performed for all the Jewish people.

argumentFlow.label: What about Hanukkah?
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara objects: Hanukkah occurred after Purim - wasn't that the 'last miracle'?

argumentFlow.label: Purim = last universal miracle
argumentFlow.text: Resolution: Purim was the last miracle for all Jews everywhere. Hanukkah affected only Jews in Eretz Yisrael. Purim remains the final universal miracle.

misconceptions: Hanukkah is the last miracle because it occurred after Purim - Purim is the last UNIVERSAL miracle (affecting all Jews); Hanukkah was local (only Eretz Yisrael).
The morning star comparison to Esther is just poetry - the Gemara uses it to make a precise historical claim about Esther's place in Jewish history.

visualizableElements.label: Jewish miracles ending with Purim
visualizableElements.description: Timeline of miracles: Exodus, Temple, Purim (last universal) - then Hanukkah (local)

visualizableElements.label: Morning star vs moonlight
visualizableElements.description: Morning star: more intense, last light before dawn. Moonlight: reflected, gentler

quizSeeds.q: How does the Gemara reconcile the claim that Esther's miracle was the last with the fact that Hanukkah came after?
quizSeeds.a: Purim was the last miracle for ALL Jews everywhere; Hanukkah was only for Jews in Eretz Yisrael. Purim is therefore the last universal Jewish miracle.

quizSeeds.q: What is the mnemonic the Gemara gives for why sinful thoughts are worse than the sin itself?
quizSeeds.a: The smell of meat drives the appetite more intensely than the taste - the mental stimulus (sinful thought) is more powerful than the act.

sugyot.finalRuling: Aggadic teachings: sinful thoughts are more spiritually damaging than the act itself; Esther's miracle (Purim) was the last universal miracle for all Jews.

display.whats: The mishna returns to the confusion incident: they slaughtered the tamid before dawn. The ruling: the incident occurred during the rest of the year (not Yom Kippur). The father of Rabbi Avin teaches: a daily offering slaughtered before dawn is invalid.
display.hint: Premature slaughter before dawn disqualifies the tamid - the daytime requirement for slaughter is absolute.

learning.summary: The mishna refers back to the confusion incident when the tamid was prematurely slaughtered. The Gemara clarifies: this occurred during the rest of the year (a regular weekday), not on Yom Kippur specifically. The father of Rabbi Avin teaches a key principle: any daily offering (tamid) slaughtered before dawn is invalid. The daytime requirement for slaughter cannot be waived even accidentally.
learning.ahaMoment: The incident's halachic consequence is clear and absolute: pre-dawn slaughter = invalid offering. There is no retroactive validation, no repair - the animal was sent to pasture and a new offering brought.
learning.memoryAnchor: Before dawn + slaughter = invalid tamid. No exceptions, no retroactive fix.
learning.openQuestion: If a tamid is accidentally slaughtered before dawn and discovered immediately, could another animal be slaughtered as a replacement, or does the error disqualify the entire day's morning service?

argumentFlow.label: Premature tamid slaughter - ruling
argumentFlow.text: The mishna addresses the confusion incident: what is the ruling when the tamid is slaughtered before dawn?

argumentFlow.label: Incident was not Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara clarifies: this incident occurred on a regular weekday, not on Yom Kippur itself.

argumentFlow.label: Father of R. Avin: pre-dawn tamid = invalid
argumentFlow.text: The father of Rabbi Avin teaches: a daily offering slaughtered before dawn is invalid - the daytime requirement is absolute.

halachic: tamid slaughter must be after dawn
narrative: clarification that incident was not Yom Kippur

misconceptions: An accidentally premature tamid can be validated retroactively - pre-dawn slaughter is an absolute invalidation with no repair.
The confusion incident happened on Yom Kippur - the Gemara clarifies it occurred on a regular weekday.

visualizableElements.label: Dawn threshold for tamid
visualizableElements.description: Before dawn: slaughter invalid. After dawn: slaughter valid. No middle ground.

quizSeeds.q: What is the ruling when the tamid is accidentally slaughtered before dawn?
quizSeeds.a: The tamid offering is invalid - pre-dawn slaughter disqualifies the offering entirely.

quizSeeds.q: During which type of day did the confusion incident (premature tamid) occur?
quizSeeds.a: On a regular weekday, not on Yom Kippur.

sugyot.finalRuling: A tamid slaughtered before dawn is invalid. The daytime requirement for slaughter is absolute.
